title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation combines a parametric sequence-to-sequence model with a non-parametric memory accessed through dense retrieval. The method improves knowledge-intensive tasks by conditioning generation on retrieved passages.
chunks: RAG couples a generator with a retriever so that factual knowledge can be fetched at inference time instead of being stored only in model parameters.
The approach marginalizes over retrieved documents and improves open-domain question answering and fact verification.
Retrieval makes generated answers easier to inspect because supporting passages are available alongside the output.

title: Dense Passage Retrieval for Open-Domain Question Answering
abstract: Dense Passage Retrieval learns dual encoders for questions and passages, enabling efficient nearest-neighbor search for open-domain question answering.
chunks: DPR replaces sparse lexical matching with dense question and passage embeddings trained from question-answer pairs.
The retriever can find relevant passages even when the exact query terms do not appear in the document.
Dense retrieval is a strong first-stage retriever for retrieval-augmented question answering systems.

title: The Probabilistic Relevance Framework: BM25 and Beyond
venue: Foundations and Trends in Information Retrieval
abstract: BM25 is a widely used probabilistic ranking function that scores documents by term frequency, inverse document frequency, and document length normalization.
chunks: BM25 rewards documents that contain query terms while controlling for overly long documents.
The method is simple, efficient, and remains a strong baseline for first-stage retrieval.
Sparse retrieval is especially useful for exact terminology, named entities, acronyms, and rare technical terms.

title: Document Expansion by Query Prediction
abstract: Doc2Query expands documents with predicted queries, improving sparse retrieval by bridging vocabulary mismatch between user queries and document text.
chunks: Document expansion adds likely user queries to documents before indexing, improving recall for sparse retrievers.
The technique addresses vocabulary mismatch without changing the retrieval engine.
Expansion can complement BM25 and is often used in multi-stage retrieval pipelines.

title: Document Ranking with a Pretrained Sequence-to-Sequence Model
abstract: MonoT5 formulates document ranking as a sequence-to-sequence task and uses a pretrained transformer to rerank candidates from a first-stage retriever.
chunks: A reranker evaluates query-document relevance after a fast first-stage retriever produces candidates.
Transformer rerankers improve ranking quality but are more expensive than sparse retrieval.
Multi-stage retrieval balances efficiency and effectiveness.

title: ColBERT: Efficient and Effective Passage Search via Contextualized Late Interaction over BERT
abstract: ColBERT uses contextualized token embeddings and late interaction to provide efficient and accurate neural passage search.
chunks: Late interaction keeps token-level representations and compares query tokens with document tokens at search time.
ColBERT improves retrieval quality while remaining more efficient than full cross-encoder reranking.
The model is useful when semantic matching needs more detail than a single vector representation.

title: Leveraging Passage Retrieval with Generative Models for Open Domain Question Answering
abstract: Fusion-in-Decoder processes multiple retrieved passages independently in the encoder and fuses evidence in the decoder for open-domain question answering.
chunks: Fusion-in-Decoder allows a generator to attend over many retrieved passages during answer generation.
The architecture highlights the importance of retrieval quality and evidence aggregation.
Using multiple passages can improve answer coverage for complex questions.

title: BEIR: A Heterogeneous Benchmark for Zero-shot Evaluation of Information Retrieval Models
abstract: BEIR evaluates retrieval models across diverse tasks and domains, exposing generalization gaps in dense retrievers.
chunks: Retrieval systems should be evaluated on heterogeneous benchmarks rather than a single dataset.
Dense models can perform unevenly in zero-shot settings, while BM25 remains a robust baseline.
Benchmarks such as BEIR motivate hybrid retrieval and careful evaluation.

title: Precise Zero-Shot Dense Retrieval without Relevance Labels
abstract: HyDE generates a hypothetical document from a query and uses its embedding for zero-shot dense retrieval, improving semantic matching without relevance labels.
chunks: HyDE expands a query into a hypothetical answer document before dense retrieval.
The generated document can bridge the gap between short queries and long evidence passages.
The idea is useful for semantic retrieval when labeled training data is unavailable.

title: Self-RAG: Learning to Retrieve, Generate, and Critique through Self-Reflection
abstract: Self-RAG trains a model to adaptively retrieve passages and critique its own generations, improving factuality and relevance.
chunks: Self-RAG retrieves only when needed and uses reflection tokens to critique generated content.
The system targets hallucination by connecting generation decisions with retrieved evidence.
Adaptive retrieval can reduce unnecessary retrieval while improving factual support.

title: Retrieval Augmentation Reduces Hallucination in Conversation
abstract: Retrieval augmentation grounds conversational generation in external knowledge and can reduce unsupported statements.
chunks: Retrieval provides grounding documents that constrain conversational responses.
Grounded generation is less likely to produce unsupported factual claims.
Evidence visibility helps users inspect why an answer was produced.

title: When Not to Trust Language Models: Investigating Effectiveness of Parametric and Non-Parametric Memories
abstract: The paper studies when language models rely on parametric memory versus retrieved knowledge and how knowledge conflicts affect factuality.
chunks: Retrieval can help update knowledge without retraining a language model.
Knowledge conflicts occur when retrieved evidence and model memory disagree.
A retrieval system should expose evidence so users can judge factual support.

title: Pretrained Transformers for Text Ranking: BERT and Beyond
abstract: This survey explains how pretrained transformers changed ad hoc retrieval, reranking, and multi-stage ranking architectures.
chunks: Transformer rankers often operate after first-stage retrieval because cross-encoders are computationally expensive.
A practical search system commonly combines sparse retrieval, dense retrieval, and neural reranking.
The survey provides context for modern IR pipelines.

title: Sentence-BERT: Sentence Embeddings using Siamese BERT-Networks
abstract: Sentence-BERT produces sentence embeddings suitable for semantic similarity search and clustering.
chunks: Sentence embeddings allow fast semantic search using vector similarity.
SBERT reduces the cost of comparing many sentence pairs compared with full BERT cross-encoding.
The method is a common upgrade path for lightweight semantic retrieval systems.

title: Billion-scale Similarity Search with GPUs
abstract: FAISS provides efficient similarity search and clustering for dense vectors, including billion-scale vector indexes.
chunks: Vector indexes make dense retrieval practical at large scale.
Approximate nearest-neighbor search trades a small amount of exactness for major efficiency gains.
A small course demo can use exact cosine similarity while leaving FAISS as a scalable extension.

title: REALM: Retrieval-Augmented Language Model Pre-Training
abstract: REALM integrates retrieval into language model pretraining so the model can retrieve documents from a large corpus while learning.
chunks: REALM treats retrieval as a latent variable during pretraining.
The model learns to use external documents for knowledge-intensive predictions.
Retrieval-augmented pretraining is an early foundation for modern RAG systems.

title: Improving Language Models by Retrieving from Trillions of Tokens
abstract: RETRO augments language models with retrieval from a massive text database, improving performance with fewer parameters.
chunks: Retrieval can improve language models by providing nearest-neighbor context from a large database.
External memory changes the scaling tradeoff between model parameters and stored knowledge.
The work shows retrieval as a practical alternative to storing all knowledge parametrically.

title: Few-shot Learning with Retrieval Augmented Language Models
abstract: Atlas combines retrieval and generation for few-shot learning and knowledge-intensive tasks.
chunks: Atlas demonstrates that retrieval can improve few-shot generalization on knowledge-intensive tasks.
The model retrieves passages and conditions generation on external evidence.
RAG systems are useful when task knowledge changes faster than model training cycles.

title: In-Context Retrieval-Augmented Language Models
abstract: In-context RALM studies how retrieved documents can be inserted into prompts for language model inference.
chunks: Retrieved documents can be placed directly into the model context without updating model weights.
Prompt-level retrieval makes RAG modular and easy to combine with existing language models.
The quality and ordering of retrieved passages strongly affects downstream generation.

title: Query Rewriting for Retrieval-Augmented Large Language Models
abstract: Query rewriting reformulates user questions into retrieval-friendly queries for retrieval-augmented language models.
chunks: Natural language questions often need rewriting before retrieval because search engines prefer explicit terms.
Query rewriting improves recall by adding missing entities, acronyms, or technical phrases.
A lightweight demo can map Chinese query terms to English IR terminology.

title: Generate Rather Than Retrieve: Large Language Models are Strong Context Generators
abstract: The paper explores using generated contexts as an alternative or supplement to retrieved passages.
chunks: Generated context can help answer questions but lacks the same evidence guarantee as retrieved documents.
Retrieval remains important when traceable sources and citations are required.
Hybrid systems can combine generated query expansion with evidence retrieval.

title: Query2Doc: Query Expansion with Large Language Models
abstract: Query2Doc expands short queries into pseudo-documents using large language models, improving sparse retrieval.
chunks: Pseudo-document expansion adds related terms to a short query before running BM25.
The approach improves recall by making lexical retrieval less brittle.
Query expansion is a practical component in academic search and RAG pipelines.

title: Large Language Models are Effective Text Rankers with Pairwise Ranking Prompting
abstract: Pairwise ranking prompting uses large language models to compare documents for a query and improve ranking quality.
chunks: LLMs can act as rerankers by comparing candidate documents pairwise.
Pairwise ranking can improve relevance ordering but costs more than first-stage retrieval.
A course demo can simulate reranking with transparent feature weights rather than calling a large model.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Large Language Models: A Survey
abstract: This survey reviews retrieval-augmented generation components, including indexing, retrieval, generation, augmentation, and evaluation.
chunks: RAG systems contain several stages: indexing, retrieval, augmentation, generation, and evaluation.
The survey organizes methods by retrieval granularity, retriever type, and generator interaction.
It is a useful reference for designing an end-to-end academic evidence finder.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for AI-Generated Content: A Survey
abstract: The survey discusses RAG applications, retriever design, augmentation methods, and evaluation concerns for AI-generated content.
chunks: RAG evaluation includes retrieval quality, generation faithfulness, citation quality, and user utility.
Evidence grounding is central for reducing hallucination in generated content.
System design should expose retrieved evidence instead of hiding it behind a final answer.

title: Reading Wikipedia to Answer Open-Domain Questions
abstract: DrQA combines document retrieval with a neural reader to answer open-domain questions from Wikipedia.
chunks: Open-domain QA often uses a retriever-reader pipeline.
A fast retriever narrows the corpus before a reader extracts or generates an answer.
This architecture anticipates later retrieval-augmented generation systems.

title: Latent Retrieval for Weakly Supervised Open Domain Question Answering
abstract: The paper learns retrieval for question answering without direct passage supervision, using answer supervision as a weak signal.
chunks: Retrieval can be trained from weak supervision when explicit relevance labels are unavailable.
Latent retrieval treats relevant documents as hidden variables.
The approach helped motivate learned retrievers for QA and RAG.

title: Real-Time Open-Domain Question Answering with Dense-Sparse Phrase Index
abstract: Dense-sparse phrase indexing supports efficient open-domain question answering with phrase-level retrieval.
chunks: Index design affects latency and retrieval granularity in QA systems.
Phrase-level retrieval can support fast extraction-oriented question answering.
Efficiency considerations matter when moving from a demo to a production retrieval service.

title: Internet-augmented Language Models through Few-shot Prompting for Open-domain Question Answering
abstract: Internet-augmented language models retrieve web documents at inference time and use them in prompts for open-domain QA.
chunks: Web retrieval can update model knowledge at inference time.
Retrieved snippets provide timely context for open-domain questions.
The method illustrates the value of separating retrieval from generation.

title: Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts
abstract: The study shows that language models often use information less effectively when relevant evidence appears in the middle of long contexts.
chunks: RAG systems should not simply stuff many retrieved chunks into a long prompt.
Passage ordering and context placement affect whether a model uses evidence.
Retrieval quality must be paired with thoughtful evidence presentation.

title: ARES: An Automated Evaluation Framework for Retrieval-Augmented Generation Systems
abstract: ARES proposes automated evaluation of RAG systems, focusing on context relevance, answer faithfulness, and answer relevance.
chunks: RAG evaluation should consider whether retrieved context is relevant and whether generated answers are faithful.
Automated judges can reduce evaluation cost but require calibration.
For a course demo, retrieval metrics such as Precision@k and MRR remain transparent and easy to report.
